# Transcrição: How_to_reduce_nerves_by_80%_when_presenting

- **Fonte:** How_to_reduce_nerves_by_80%_when_presenting
- **Duração:** 03:06
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Then how do I deal with the nerves, the bad nerves, the bad kind? I want you to relax because the answer is actually really simple. But simple doesn't mean easy. The answer to this question comes in two parts. The first part, you have to firstly understand that fear comes from the unknown. Anxiety comes from the unknown. When you don't know what's coming next, that's when you feel anxiety. So what's the key thing you have to know when you're giving a presentation? What you're going to say. So all of a sudden when you haven't memorized or you haven't learned what you're going to say, really well, then naturally you're going to feel fear. Naturally you're going to feel anxiety. So you guessed it. The first thing you have to know inside out is your content. And I really mean you have to know it inside out. Most people, what they do when they're learning their speeches, they read it internally in their heads maybe three or four times. And that just doesn't cut it. Because the first thing they did wrong is you shouldn't practice internally in your head. When you're reading your speech, you have to read it out loud as if you're presenting to an audience. Because in that sense, you're not only rehearsing the content. You need to rehearse the delivery, but more on that after. So then the next question is, well, Vin, how long should I rehearse for? And you know, I come from the world of theater. So the rule of thumb in theater is that for every minute you are presenting, for every minute you are on stage, there's two hours of rehearsal attached to that minute. And I know what you're thinking. You're thinking, that's crazy, Vin. I'm not going to rehearse 60 hours for a 30 minute presentation. And you're right, you don't have to do 60 hours. But if you want to crush it, you should. If it's a very impactful presentation that you have to make and your whole career and life may hang on that presentation, then hell yeah, put 60 hours into it. But if it's just a presentation you give on a weekly basis, I still believe you should be putting at least 10 hours into a presentation. So let's say you're spending 10 hours to rehearse for a 30 minute presentation. What happens when you do the first five hours of rehearsal? During those first five hours, what are you rehearsing? You're rehearsing the content. You're getting used to the content. This is when you know it inside out. This is when you begin to know it back to front. This is when you gain the confidence of no matter where you are in the presentation, you know exactly where you are and you know exactly what comes next. You have no idea how powerful this is. So many people go, oh, rehearsing for 10 hours. Oh, I don't really want to. But you have no idea, you literally have no idea how much of the bad nerves this eliminates. It gets rid of at least 50% of the bad nerves that you feel before a presentation. Now, what's going to get rid of the next, say, 30% of the anxiety and fear? It's actually rehearsing again. Yet this time you're not rehearsing the content. You're rehearsing the delivery. Because remember, fear comes from the unknown. And it's not about just not knowing what comes next in terms of content. A lot of the times people don't know what and how they're going to say, what they're going to say. So when you don't know how you're going to say what you're going to say, meaning delivery, this also creates some level of anxiety too. I hope you can see what we're doing here. All we're doing is we're making all of the unknowns known.

## [03:00 - 03:06]

And the more of the unknowns we make known, the less nervous and the less fear we feel.
